answers.answer: The in-scope protected-write surface is the full five-route POST set selected by `resolve_write_route_category(...)`: `POST /v1/markets`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/comments`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/resolve`, `POST /v1/markets/{id}/orders/probability-edit`, and `POST /v1/markets/{id}/orders/event-trade`. Unsupported POST paths stay outside the rate-limit and quota-header contract.
answers.uncertainty: The checkout's task artifact path differs from the prompt suffix, but the parent task plan and the shared classifier agree on the same five-route boundary.

answers.answer: Keep emitting `X-RateLimit-*` headers only on successful protected-write responses and on `429 rate_limit_exceeded`. `BayesHandler.handle_api()` adds `rate_limit_headers(agent_id)` only when `status < 400`, and only attaches quota headers on failures for the `rate_limit_exceeded` branch. The parent task plan freezes that shared contract and explicitly does not broaden `400` or `409` application errors. The backend audit did not expose a gap in this seam.
answers.uncertainty: The only mismatch I found is in tests, not the backend: resolve success still has a stale header-absence expectation.

answers.answer: Protected POSTs should continue consuming quota before route-specific idempotency replay or terminal rejection logic. `_enforce_write_controls()` calls `enforce_rate_limit()` before routing, and each protected handler checks idempotency or terminal rejection after that point. The parent plan freezes that ordering instead of special-casing replays or rejected writes. Under the current shared adapter, successful replays can surface the updated quota snapshot, while rejected `409` responses still consume quota but omit headers because they are not success or `429` responses.
answers.uncertainty: There is no dedicated quota-accounting test for replayed or rejected protected writes; this answer comes from call order plus the replay and rejection contract tests.

answers.answer: Keep `X-RateLimit-Policy` as part of the contract. `rate_limit_headers(...)` always includes it, the auth/rate-limit test helpers assert it on presence and absence paths, and the parent task plan explicitly rejects narrowing the surface to only `Limit`, `Remaining`, and `Reset`.
answers.uncertainty: None beyond an explicit future product decision to narrow the public header set.

answers.answer: Yes. Protected write routes should keep omitting quota headers when `RATE_LIMIT_PER_MIN <= 0` or when no normalized `X-Bayes-Agent-Id` is available. Both `enforce_rate_limit()` and `rate_limit_headers()` no-op in those cases, and the existing ProbabilityEdit HTTP tests already freeze the omission behavior. The parent plan keeps that omission unchanged across the rest of the protected POST surface.
answers.uncertainty: Current explicit absence assertions only cover ProbabilityEdit, but the shared adapter and helper logic make the same omission apply to the other protected POST routes.

answers.answer: The minimum expected test matrix is route-breadth success-path header coverage for all five protected POST routes, while keeping the existing ProbabilityEdit tests as the shared proof for remaining-quota decrement, `429`, `Retry-After`, and per-agent isolation. The shared adapter already emits success-path headers across the protected surface, so the main gap is breadth in `tests/test_bayes_market_api.py`: ProbabilityEdit already has the full header matrix, resolve still has a stale success-path absence assertion, create-market has auth coverage but no success header assertion, and comment-post plus event-trade need first-class header checks. This audit does not indicate a backend patch unless those new breadth tests fail.
answers.uncertainty: Comment-post currently has no auth/rate-limit-specific HTTP coverage, so the exact helper shape is still an implementation detail rather than a frozen testing API.

assumption_checks.impact: Implementation and tests should target exactly the five POST routes resolved by `resolve_write_route_category(...)`; unsupported POST paths should stay outside the quota-header contract.
The shared `BayesHandler.handle_api()` plus `rate_limit_headers(...)` path is the right implementation boundary. The backend audit did not show a reason to add route-local header plumbing.
Anonymous requests and disabled-limiter mode should keep omitting quota headers across the protected write surface unless someone explicitly changes the contract.
The frontend currently sends `X-Bayes-Agent-Id` but does not read response headers, so this task can stay backend-and-tests only.
Keep `X-RateLimit-Policy` unless there is an explicit API-contract narrowing; removing it would require coordinated helper and test updates.
